Africa

Grant's Zebra

Equus quagga boehmi
Status Near Threatened
Habitat Savannahs, Grasslands
Diet Herbivore
Lifespan 20-30 years
Weight 220-450 kg
×

Grant's zebra (Equus quagga boehmi) represents the most widespread and recognizable subspecies of plains zebra, distinguished by its narrow, closely spaced stripes that extend unbroken across the belly. Native to East Africa's iconic savannas, these striking equids form one of the most visually spectacular components of the region's wildlife. Their distinctive black-and-white patterns serve multiple evolutionary purposes, from confusing predators to repelling biting insects. As a keystone species of the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem, Grant's zebras participate in one of the world's largest wildlife migrations alongside wildebeest and gazelles.

Habitat & Range

Grant's zebras inhabit the savannas and grasslands of East Africa, with their range centered on the Serengeti-Mara ecosystem spanning Tanzania and Kenya. They prefer open plains and grasslands with access to water sources, avoiding dense woodland. Their habitat requirements overlap extensively with wildebeest, facilitating the mixed-species migrations. Key strongholds include Serengeti National Park, Ngorongoro Conservation Area, Masai Mara National Reserve, and Tsavo ecosystem. They have been extirpated from much of their historic range outside these protected areas.

Behavior & Social Structure

Grant's zebras are highly social animals living in stable family groups consisting of a single stallion, several mares, and their offspring. These harems maintain strong bonds and occupy exclusive home ranges. Bachelor males form loose associations until able to challenge established stallions. Zebras communicate through a complex repertoire of vocalizations, ear positions, and tail movements. Their famous stripes serve social purposes—individuals recognize each other by pattern. They also form mutualistic relationships with wildebeest during migration, benefiting from the latter's superior ability to detect predators.

Diet & Nutrition

As grazers, Grant's zebras primarily feed on grasses, consuming up to 8 kg of vegetation daily. Their digestive system allows them to process coarse, low-quality grasses that many other herbivores cannot digest, reducing competition. During the wet season, they prefer fresh green grasses, while in the dry season they rely on tougher, more fibrous vegetation. They require daily access to water and typically drink at least once per day. Their grazing behavior helps maintain grassland ecosystems by preventing any single grass species from dominating.

Key Facts

  • Most widespread subspecies of plains zebra
  • Unique stripe pattern acts as individual fingerprint
  • Stripes create optical illusion that confuses predators during chase
  • Research shows stripes help repel biting tsetse flies
  • Can run at speeds up to 65 km/h to escape predators
  • Forms part of Great Migration with 1.5+ million wildebeest

Conservation Status

Classified as Near Threatened by the IUCN Red List. Grant's zebra populations have declined significantly in recent decades due to habitat loss, hunting for skins, and human-wildlife conflict. While still relatively abundant in protected areas like the Serengeti and Masai Mara, their range has contracted considerably outside these strongholds. Conservation efforts focus on maintaining habitat connectivity between protected areas, anti-poaching measures, and community-based programs that reduce conflict with livestock grazing.

Primary Threats

  • Habitat loss and fragmentation from agriculture and settlement
  • Hunting for skins and bushmeat
  • Human-wildlife conflict with livestock grazing
  • Competition with domestic livestock for water and forage
  • Drought and climate change affecting food and water availability

Related Species

🦒 Maasai Giraffe 🐘 African Elephant 🦁 Lion

Frequently Asked Questions

How big is an Grant's Zebra?

Grant's Zebras typically weigh 220-450 kg and can be identified by their distinctive features. They are native to Savannahs, Grasslands.

Are Grant's Zebras endangered?

The Grant's Zebra is currently classified as Near Threatened by the IUCN Red List. Classified as Near Threatened by the IUCN Red List. Grant's zebra populations have declined significantly in recent decades due to habitat loss, hunti...

What do Grant's Zebras eat?

Grant's Zebras are herbivore. As grazers, Grant's zebras primarily feed on grasses, consuming up to 8 kg of vegetation daily. Their digestive system a...

How long do Grant's Zebras live?

In the wild, Grant's Zebras typically live for 20-30 years. Their lifespan can vary based on environmental conditions and conservation efforts.
